hero.title: I build tools that make teams autonomous
hero.subtitle: Fullstack & DevOps at ESLC since 2022. Specialized in rapidly shipping robust business applications: HRIS, data dashboards, API automation.

about.title: Four years industrializing internal tools
about.p1: Apprentice at Groupe ESLC since September 2022, and freelancer in parallel. My playground: the internal tools that didn't exist, or that needed to be rebuilt properly.
about.p2: Concretely, I take a project from A to Z — architecture, front and back development, deployment, docs and follow-up. Solo if needed, in full autonomy.

stack.title: The tools I use day to day

hero.title: Degrés Jours — Automated weather pipeline
hero.pitch: Daily weather data collection chain for 22 ESLC sites. A C# script triggers everything at 1:30 PM; by 1:45 PM the files are consumed by ICopitole — leaving a 10-minute intervention window.

context.title: A daily operational challenge
context.p1: Groupe ESLC operates 22 sites across France. Every day, degree days — a key thermal indicator for heating and air conditioning — must be collected from MétéoFrance and injected into ICopitole, the group's energy management software.
context.p2: Before this project, collection was manual. I designed a fully automated pipeline: a C# script (SCR-01) runs every day at 1:30 PM, collects data from 22 stations, and writes it in parallel to .data files (for ICopitole) and MongoDB (for the real-time web portal).

timeline.title: The critical 10-minute window
timeline.clickHint: Click a step to see details

nominal.detail: The Windows scheduled task launches SCR-01. The script queries the MétéoFrance API for each of the 22 sites and collects yesterday's TN/TX data.

error.detail: The Windows scheduled task launches SCR-01. The script queries the MétéoFrance API — one or more stations return an error (timeout, missing data, API quota exceeded).

nominal.detail: SCR-01 completes its run. A summary email is sent automatically: 22/22 stations collected, .data files written to SRV21 and SRV22, MongoDB up to date.

error.detail: SCR-01 detects an anomaly and sends an alert email: failed stations listed, .data files partial or missing. The 10-minute intervention window starts now.

nominal.detail: Everything went smoothly. ICopitole will consume the files at 1:45 PM. Nothing to do.

error.detail: Last chance before the point of no return. An operator can correct missing data and manually re-trigger collection for failed stations via the admin portal.

nominal.detail: The ICopitole scheduled task runs. It reads the .data files present on SRV21 and SRV22 and integrates them into the energy management database. Pipeline complete.

error.detail: ICopitole consumes whatever is present in the .data files, even if incomplete. This is the point of no return: after this time, a correction requires manually re-running the downstream ICopitole tasks.

nominal.action: Data available on the web portal
nominal.detail: MongoDB data (populated in parallel by SCR-01) has been available on the web portal since 1:35 PM. The pipeline is complete and today's degree days are displayed.

error.detail: If a correction was made after 1:45 PM, the downstream ICopitole tasks must be re-run manually to propagate the corrected data. The web portal can be updated independently via MongoDB.

decision.title: Parallel dual-write instead of a queue
decision.content: SCR-01 writes simultaneously to .data files AND MongoDB. A queue could have decoupled the two destinations — but ICopitole consumes a legacy file format that's non-negotiable, and MongoDB serves the real-time portal. Coupling both via a queue would have added an extra failure point for zero benefit: if the queue goes down, both destinations go down. With direct dual-write, a MongoDB error doesn't prevent ICopitole from running, and vice versa.

stations.title: 22 MétéoFrance stations
stations.description: For each ESLC site, the nearest active MétéoFrance station with available daily TN/TX data.
